title: Portugal
dek: Sun-soaked tiles, Atlantic seafood, and hilltop castles — a practical guide to Portugal's capital and a day trip to fairy-tale Sintra.
footer: Location photos via Wikimedia Commons — tap any credit for its source, author and licence.
verified: ✓ Verified June 2026 — reconfirm prices, hours & transport fares before you go
intro: Lisbon is one of Europe's most walkable and affordable capitals: easy to navigate by tram and metro, generous with free viewpoints, and endlessly rewarding on foot through the Alfama's tiled alleys.

sections.title: When you land
sections.body: Humberto Delgado Airport (LIS) is inside the city — the Metro is the easiest way in:
checklist: Buy a Viva Viagem card at any Metro ticket machine (€0.50 for the reusable card)
Load credit or a 24-hour pass (€7.25) and take the Red Line toward São Sebastião (~20–25 min to Baixa-Chiado area; change at Alameda if needed)
Alternatively, Uber or taxi to the centre runs ≈€15–20 and takes ~20–25 min
Text or call your accommodation — many Lisbon streets are narrow and one-way

sections.title: Local essentials
sections.body: Plug: Type F (two round pins), 230 V / 50 Hz — US devices need an adapter and a voltage check. Time zone: WET/WEST (UTC+0 in winter, UTC+1 in summer — same as London, not the same as Spain). Tap water: safe and good everywhere in Lisbon — carry a reusable bottle. Pharmacies show a green cross; a rotation system means there is always at least one open 24/7 nearby — check farmaciasdeservico.net for the current duty pharmacy in your area.

sections.title: Entry & documents
sections.body: ⚠️ The rules below assume a US passport. Other nationalities should check their own requirements.
checklist: Visa-free for US citizens — up to 90 days in any 180-day period in the Schengen area
Passport must be valid for at least 3 months beyond your departure date
ETIAS not yet required as of June 2026, but is expected to launch later in 2026 — check before you book (≈€20 fee when live)
EU Type F plug adapter — one strip can charge everything
Travel insurance covering medical care abroad — strongly recommended

sections.title: Booking checklist
sections.body: Roughly in this order — accommodation fills fast in summer (July–August prices jump 40–60%):
checklist: Flights, round-trip — ≈$500–1,000 from the US East Coast (book early)
Accommodation — book 2–3 months ahead for summer; budget hotel ≈€80–150/night
Jerónimos Monastery timed entry (book online to skip the queue) — €10
Pena Palace, Sintra — book online; queues without a reservation are long — ≈€14
Travel insurance (a few weeks out)
Viva Viagem Metro card (buy on arrival at the airport)
Offline maps for Lisbon + Sintra (week before)

sections.title: Phone & data
sections.body: Portugal is in the EU, so EU-roaming SIMs (or plans that include EU data) work at no extra charge. US travellers have several options: eSIM from providers like Airalo or Holafly (≈$5–15 for 5–10 GB) is the easiest; a local SIM from NOS, MEO or Vodafone PT at the airport or city shops; or your carrier's international day-pass if you have one. Coverage in Lisbon and Sintra is excellent; rural Alentejo can be patchy.

sections.title: Holidays & your dates
sections.body: Portugal's main summer public holidays: June 10 (Portugal / Camões Day) — expect some closures and parades; August 15 (Assumption) — many smaller shops closed. Lisbon's own holiday is June 13 (Santo António), the city's biggest street party — Alfama is mobbed with grilled sardines, street bands and dancing; great fun, but book restaurants well in advance. No public holidays in July or September. Peak season is July–August: crowded, expensive, and very hot (often 35 °C+); June and September are better value and still warm.

sections.title: Money & currency
sections.body: Portugal uses the euro (€). Mid-2026 rate: €1 ≈ $1.16 (approximate — check before you go). Cards are accepted almost everywhere in Lisbon; carry some cash for small local tascas, market stalls and tipping. Tipping: not compulsory, but appreciated — round up at cafés, or leave 5–10% at sit-down restaurants, in cash if possible (card tip prompts are rare). ATMs are plentiful; use your bank's network to avoid high fees.

sections.title: Budget & daily costs
sections.intro: Rough per-person, mid-range estimates in euros for a 7-day Lisbon trip. Lisbon is one of Western Europe's best-value capitals — you can eat and drink very well without spending much. Tap a field to log your real spend; the total saves on this device.

items.label: Accommodation, per night
items.note: Budget hotel ≈€80–150; hostels from ≈€25. Prices spike in July–August.

items.label: Food & drink, per day
items.note: Cheap set lunch (prato do dia) ≈€8–12; dinner ≈€15–25 pp; coffee €0.80–1.20.

items.label: Sights & activities, per day
items.note: Many viewpoints are free. Major paid sights: Belém Tower €6, São Jorge €10, Jerónimos €10. First Sunday of the month: state museums free.

items.label: Flights — round trip
items.note: ≈$500–1,000 USD from the US East Coast — book early.

items.label: Sintra day trip (train + Pena Palace)
items.note: Train from Rossio ≈€2.30 each way + Pena Palace entry ≈€14. Book the palace online.

sections.title: Health & pharmacy
sections.body:
Emergencies: dial 112 (free) for ambulance, fire or police — English-speaking operators available. For a non-emergency medical question, call 808 24 24 24 (24/7 health line). Non-emergency police is also reachable via 112.

Pharmacies (farmácias) show a green cross. Portugal uses a duty-rotation system — there is always one open 24 hours nearby, but the location rotates. Check farmaciasdeservico.net or ask your hotel. As a US visitor you have no Portuguese health card; private emergency treatment is available but not free — travel insurance covering medical care is strongly recommended. Tap water is safe to drink throughout Lisbon.
checklist: Save 112 (all emergencies) and 808 24 24 24 (non-emergency health line)
Confirm your travel insurance covers medical treatment abroad
Pack medicines in original packaging, plus copies of any prescriptions
Sunscreen — Lisbon sun is intense June–September; UV index regularly hits 8–10
Find your nearest duty pharmacy at farmaciasdeservico.net

sections.title: Etiquette & language
sections.body:
English is widely spoken in Lisbon, especially in hospitality, tourism and among younger locals — you will rarely struggle. A few words of Portuguese are warmly received: olá (hello), obrigado / obrigada (thank you, male / female speaker), por favor (please), com licença (excuse me, passing through), onde fica…? (where is…?).

Tipping: not expected but appreciated — round up at cafés, leave 5–10% at sit-down restaurants in cash. Trams: let people off before boarding, and move to the back — tram 28 is very cramped. Mealtimes: lunch runs 12:30–15:00; dinner rarely starts before 20:00 — arriving at 19:00 marks you as a tourist. Sundays: some smaller shops close or keep short hours. Haggling: not a norm.

sections.title: Key transit routes
steps: Airport → centre: Red Metro line from Aeroporto toward Oriente, change at Alameda for the Green line to Baixa-Chiado (~25 min total) — or Uber/taxi ≈€15–20.

Around the city: The Metro (4 lines, €1.90/ride or €7.25/day) covers most neighbourhoods quickly. Tram 28 (historic yellow tram) runs through Alfama and Estrela — iconic but slow and packed; validate your pass before boarding.
Belém (museums + tower): Tram 15E or bus 727 from Praça do Comércio to Belém (~20 min). Alternatively a short Uber.

To Sintra (day trip): CP train from Rossio Station (or Oriente) to Sintra (~40 min, ≈€2.30 each way) — validate your ticket at the platform scanner. Trains run roughly every 20 min. Book Pena Palace online in advance.
Across the Tagus (Cacilhas): Ferry from Cais do Sodré every ~15 min (≈€1.55 each way, ~10 min crossing) for views of the river and the 25 de Abril Bridge.
Getting a taxi/Uber: Uber works well in Lisbon and is usually cheaper than metered taxis; confirm your pickup point clearly as many streets are pedestrianised.

items.title: Arrival + Baixa & Chiado
items.body: Arrive, collect your Viva Viagem card, and check in. Spend the afternoon wandering Baixa (the grid-plan lower town) and Chiado (the literary café district). Evening drink at a miradouro — Portas do Sol or Santa Catarina — with views over the Tagus.
checklist: Viva Viagem card loaded
Offline maps downloaded

items.title: Alfama & São Jorge Castle
items.body: Morning at Castelo de São Jorge for panoramic views, then wander down through Alfama's tiled alleys. Look for a tasca serving prato do dia for lunch. Afternoon at the National Tile Museum (Museu do Azulejo) — one of Portugal's best; ≈€5 entry.
checklist: São Jorge Castle entry ≈€10 (book online to skip queue)
Wear comfortable shoes — Alfama is steep and cobbled

items.title: Belém — towers, monastery, pastéis
items.body: Take tram 15E to Belém. See the UNESCO Jerónimos Monastery (book timed entry online — €10), then the Torre de Belém on the riverfront (€6). Lunch at Pastéis de Belém for the original custard tart — queue moves fast. Walk the riverside promenade in the afternoon.
checklist: Jerónimos timed entry booked online
Arrive at Pastéis de Belém by 11:30 or after 15:00 to avoid peak queues

items.title: Sintra day trip
items.pace: Full day — hilly; wear good shoes
items.body: CP train from Rossio to Sintra (~40 min). Bus 434 from Sintra station loops to Pena Palace (colourful Romantic hilltop palace — ≈€14, book online), Moorish Castle and back. Afternoon in Sintra town: Quinta da Regaleira gardens, local pastries (travesseiros). Return train to Lisbon by early evening.
checklist: Pena Palace entry booked online
Validate train ticket at platform scanner
Bus 434 runs on a loop — check last timings back to Sintra station

items.title: LX Factory + Príncipe Real
items.body: Morning at LX Factory (best on Sundays for the big market — other days more atmospheric and quieter). Afternoon in Príncipe Real: independent boutiques, the covered market, and the Jardim do Príncipe Real. Evening: Time Out Market for dinner — every major Lisbon chef has a counter here.
items.note: LX Factory Sunday market runs 10:00–19:00 — the week's best version.
checklist: LX Factory is free to enter; budget €10–20 for food and browsing

items.title: Free day — coast or culture
items.body: Two good options: the Estoril/Cascais train line from Cais do Sodré for a morning at the beach (≈30 min, ≈€2.30 each way), or a slower museum day — Museu Calouste Gulbenkian (world-class art collection, ≈€10) in Lisbon. Evening: fado dinner in Alfama (book ahead for a restaurant with live fado).
checklist: Fado dinner — book at least a day ahead in peak season

items.title: Departure day
items.body: Last pastel de nata with a coffee. Red Metro line from central Lisbon to Aeroporto (~20–25 min) — allow 2 hours before your flight for international departures. No VAT refund scheme for most travellers (Portugal is EU); if flying to a non-EU destination, check at the airport.
checklist: Metro Red line to Aeroporto — validate your ticket
Allow 2 hr before flight for check-in + security

items.name: Torre de Belém
items.body: A 16th-century Manueline tower on the Tagus riverfront — ornate stonework, watchtowers and river views. UNESCO World Heritage. Entry ≈€6; book online to avoid queues. Open Tue–Sun 10:00–17:30 (18:30 in summer); closed Mon.

img.alt: Torre de Belém on the Tagus, Lisbon

items.name: Mosteiro dos Jerónimos
items.body: An enormous, intricately carved monastery built to celebrate Vasco da Gama's voyage to India — one of Portugal's greatest buildings. UNESCO World Heritage. Entry ≈€10; book a timed slot online. Open Tue–Sun 09:00–18:00 (17:00 Oct–Apr); closed Mon.

items.name: Castelo de São Jorge
items.body: A Moorish castle rebuilt by medieval Portuguese kings, perched over the Alfama with 360° views of the city and river. Entry ≈€10. Open daily 09:00–21:00 (18:00 Nov–Feb). Book online to avoid the queue.

items.name: LX Factory
items.kicker: Creative hub in a former textile mill
items.body: A cluster of independent restaurants, bars, bookshops and studios in a 19th-century riverside factory. Free to enter. Best visited on Sunday (10:00–19:00) for the market; lively any evening. Home to the beloved bookshop Ler Devagar.

items.name: Alfama
items.body: Lisbon's oldest neighbourhood — a labyrinth of tiled alleys, laundry lines, and neighbourhood tascas spilling down from the castle. No single entrance; just walk in from Portas do Sol. Home to fado's roots.

items.name: Pastéis de Belém
items.body: The birthplace of the pastel de nata, made to a secret recipe since 1837. Eat them warm with cinnamon at the counter — €1.20–1.50 each. Queue moves fast; come before 11:30 or after 15:00. Rua de Belém 84–92.

sections.title: Free viewpoints (miradouros)
sections.body: Lisbon's best views cost nothing. Portas do Sol and Santa Luzia overlook the Alfama's rooftops and the Tagus — both are on the tram 28 route. Graça (miradouro da Graça) is a local favourite with fewer tourists. Santa Catarina (Miradouro de Santa Catarina) faces the river and the 25 de Abril Bridge — a classic sunset spot. None charge admission.

items.name: Palácio Nacional da Pena, Sintra
items.body: A wildly colourful Romantic palace perched above the clouds on a forested hill — the main reason to come to Sintra. Entry ≈€14 (palace + gardens); book online. Bus 434 from Sintra station runs on a loop to the palace and back. Also worth seeing: Quinta da Regaleira and the ruins of the Moorish Castle.

sections.title: What to eat
sections.body:
Portugal punches well above its weight for food. Don't miss: pastel de nata (custard tart — warm, with cinnamon); bifana (spiced pork sandwich, ≈€3); prego (steak sandwich); bacalhau (salt cod — claimed 365 recipes); grilled sardines (especially around the June Santo António festival); and polvo à lagareiro (octopus with olive oil and garlic). Wash it down with a Super Bock or a glass of vinho verde. Coffee: a bica is an espresso; a galão is the milky equivalent of a latte — and at €0.80–1.20, a bargain.

For dinner with everything under one roof, Time Out Market (Mercado da Ribeira, Cais do Sodré) has counters from the city's top chefs — no reservation needed, open daily. For a more local experience, look for a tasca serving prato do dia (dish of the day with bread, salad and a drink for ≈€8–12).

sections.title: Shopping
sections.body: Rua Augusta and the Baixa grid are the main shopping streets — international chains plus some local ceramics shops. Better hunting: Príncipe Real for independent boutiques, vintage and design; LX Factory for books, concept stores and local brands; Feira da Ladra flea market (Campo de Santa Clara, Tue & Sat mornings) for antiques, tiles and vintage prints. For quality ceramics and azulejos (tiles), look for reputable shops rather than tourist stalls — prices vary hugely. Portugal does no VAT refund for EU-bound travellers; if flying to a non-EU country, ask at shops and claim at the airport.

sections.title: Souvenirs worth bringing home
items: Hand-painted azulejo (tile) — buy from a reputable ceramics shop, not a stall
Pastéis de nata — eat them there; they don't travel well
A bottle of ginjinha (cherry liqueur) or port wine
Vinho verde (green wine) — light, slightly sparkling, cheap
Portuguese cork products (wallets, bags) — Portugal produces half the world's cork
Sardine tins — they sell them like artworks, in beautifully designed packaging
